items.label: Flights — the open-jaw
items.body: Fly into Edinburgh (EDI) and home from Inverness (INV). It costs a little more than a return into the same airport and it saves an entire day of backtracking down the A9 — worth it. Book 5–6 months out for late May; expect ~CAD 1,400–1,700 return. Inverness usually connects through London, Amsterdam or Dublin.

items.label: Why late May and early June
items.body: It's the driest stretch of the year on the west coast, daylight runs from about 4:45am to past 10pm (and never goes fully dark in Assynt by June), the midges are only just waking up, and the summer crowds haven't arrived — school holidays start in July. The Edinburgh Fringe is in August, so the city in May is simply a city. This is the window Scots keep for themselves.

items.label: The car — and the ferries
items.body: No car for the first five days: Edinburgh runs on trams, buses and your feet, and driving in the Old Town is a punishment. Pick the car up at Edinburgh airport on day six — you'll be on the M9 within minutes, which is the gentlest possible place to get used to driving on the left. Take a COMPACT car: the Drumbeg road (B869) and the Hushinish road (B887) are genuine single-track, and a big car turns them into work. Two CalMac crossings carry the car: Uig → Tarbert (Harris) and Stornoway → Ullapool.

items.label: Book the ferry the day sales open
items.body: This is the single most time-critical thing in the whole trip. The Uig–Tarbert car deck sells out months in advance for summer sailings, and CalMac typically opens the following year's bookings around the third Friday of January. Put a calendar reminder on it. Note also: that ferry does NOT sail on Sundays (Sabbath observance is still real on Harris and Lewis), which is why this itinerary crosses on a Monday morning with a transit night at Uig on the Skye side.

items.label: Single-track driving, honestly
items.body: Passing places are for passing, not parking. Whoever is nearest one pulls in — and if the nearest is on your right, you stop and wait for the oncoming car to reach it. Reversing a hundred metres into a passing place is normal here, not a failure. Wave at every driver who pulls in for you; it's close to mandatory. Sheep have no traffic sense whatsoever and will claim the road on Harris and in Coigach. Slow down well before blind crests.

items.label: Fuel and groceries — plan ahead
items.body: Petrol stations are genuinely scarce out west. Fill up before Cowal (at Arrochar or Strachur — the Tighnabruaich village pump's status is uncertain), fill up at Ardhasaig or Leverburgh on Harris before the Hushinish run, and fill up in Lochinver before heading out to Coigach, which has no fuel at all. Groceries: Tarbert Co-op on Harris (closed Sundays) and Lochinver Stores in Assynt (the SPAR, also the post office and the cash machine).

items.label: Sunday on Harris and Lewis
items.body: The Outer Hebrides are the last stronghold of Sabbath observance in the UK: shops shut, the distillery shuts, most restaurants shut, and the Uig ferry doesn't sail. It reshapes plans, and it also empties the island further — a Sunday is a genuinely wonderful day for Luskentyre or the Golden Road, precisely because everything human-run has stopped. In this itinerary the Sunday problem is solved upstream: the crossing happens on the Monday.

items.label: Money and phones
items.body: Pounds sterling. Cards work almost everywhere, but carry some cash for the honesty box at Croft36 and the smallest seafood shacks. Cash machines thin out fast: use Tarbert or Leverburgh on Harris, Lochinver in Assynt. Mobile signal is patchy across the west and the islands (EE has the widest rural footprint) — download offline maps before you leave Edinburgh, and do not rely on live data on the single-tracks.

items.label: Eating — how to choose
items.body: The rule holds here as anywhere: if the menu is translated into six languages and has photographs, walk away. In Scotland the honest signals are a blackboard that changes daily, a hatch instead of a maître d', and a queue of people who are clearly not on holiday. The best meals of this trip come off a counter (the Seafood Shack), out of a shed with an honesty box (Croft36), or from a kitchen that only cooks what the day-boats landed (Dulse, North Harbour Bistro). Book the two set-menu nights — Scarista House and the Kilberry Inn — weeks ahead; both have very few tables.

items.label: Midges, weather, layers
items.body: Early June sits right at the start of the midge season — present, building, not yet in charge. Pack Smidge (made for Scottish midges), avoid lingering at dusk beside still water, and favour breezy coastal spots. Weather: 12–17°C, changeable within the hour, and a wind that finds you on every beach. Layers, a proper waterproof shell, and shoes you don't mind soaking. Sunscreen too — seventeen hours of daylight adds up faster than anyone expects.

items.label: Booking windows, in order
items.body: 1) CalMac car deck, the day 2027 sales open (January). 2) Harris accommodation — the island has very few beds and the good ones go 6–12 months out; the Rock House at Borve especially. 3) Scarista House and the Kilberry Inn (few tables, one seating). 4) The Fingal in Leith (23 cabins) and the Portobello sauna. 5) Everything else, two to four weeks ahead. Re-verify opening days for the small places a week before you travel: out here, seasonal hours move.
